source: Van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma.
description: Body-centered practices for releasing stored tension, processing trauma, and reconnecting with physical sensations. Based on understanding that trauma and emotion are held in the body and can be released through movement, breath, and somatic awareness.

strategies.name: Tension Release Exercises (TRE)
strategies.description: Series of exercises that intentionally create muscle fatigue to trigger the body's natural tremoring mechanism for releasing deep muscular patterns of stress and tension.
steps: Stand with feet hip-width apart
Exercise 1: Raise onto toes for 1-2 minutes (calf fatigue)
Exercise 2: Deep squat hold against wall for 1-2 minutes (leg fatigue)
Exercise 3: Gentle leg stretches to further activate tremor reflex
Lie on back, knees bent, feet together, knees apart (butterfly position)
Let legs gently tremor/shake - this is the body's natural release
Don't control or stop the tremoring - allow it
Tremors may spread through hips, pelvis, torso - this is normal
Practice for 5-15 minutes (build up gradually)
To stop: bring knees together, straighten legs, rest
Stay lying down for a few minutes after, notice how body feels
May feel tired, relaxed, emotional - all normal responses

citations.author: Berceli, D.
citations.title: Trauma Releasing Exercises (TRE): A revolutionary new method for stress/trauma recovery

strategies.contraindications: Pregnancy, recent surgery, severe PTSD without therapist support. Start very gradually. Can be activating.

strategies.name: Shaking/Vibration Practice
strategies.description: Intentional full-body shaking to discharge stress, release stuck energy, and reset the nervous system.
steps: Stand with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent and loose
Begin bouncing gently, letting knees be soft and springy
Let the bounce move through your whole body
Add gentle shaking of hands and arms
Gradually let the shaking intensify and spread
Shake arms, shoulders, torso, hips, legs - whole body
Let jaw be loose, facial muscles soft
Breath naturally - don't hold breath
Make sound if it wants to come out - sighs, groans, etc.
Continue for 3-10 minutes
Gradually slow down, come to stillness
Stand still and notice sensations: tingling, warmth, aliveness
Can be done to music for added energy

citations.author: Bradford, K.
citations.title: The Shake: An Embodied Practice for Transformation

strategies.contraindications: May feel silly initially; some people find it activating. Listen to your body.

strategies.name: Ecstatic/Expressive Dance
strategies.description: Free-form movement to music without choreography, allowing the body to express and release emotions through dance.
steps: Choose a private space where you won't be interrupted
Select music that calls to you (start with something energizing)
Close eyes or soften gaze
Begin moving however your body wants to move
No right or wrong way - follow your body's impulses
Let movement be authentic, not 'pretty' or 'good'
Express whatever emotions arise through movement
Let arms swing, hips sway, feet stomp - whatever feels right
Build energy, then let it shift - soft to powerful, slow to fast
Include all body parts - even face, hands, fingers
Let sound come out if it wants to - sighs, grunts, cries
Dance for 15-45 minutes
End with slower music, gentle movements
Rest in stillness, notice what you feel

citations.author: Roth, G.
citations.title: Sweat Your Prayers: The Five Rhythms of the Soul

strategies.contraindications: Requires privacy; may bring up strong emotions. Start with shorter sessions.

strategies.name: Trauma-Sensitive Yoga
strategies.description: Gentle yoga practice emphasizing interoception, choice, and safety to support trauma recovery.
steps: Create a safe environment - dim lights, comfortable temperature
Begin in a comfortable position (seated or lying down)
Close eyes only if that feels safe; otherwise soft gaze
Take several breaths, noticing body sensations
Move into simple poses very slowly
Key principle: YOU are in charge - modify or skip any pose
Notice sensations without judgment: 'I notice tightness', 'I notice warmth'
Suggested poses: child's pose, cat-cow, gentle twists, legs up the wall
Hold each pose 5-10 breaths or until you want to change
If dissociation occurs, open eyes, look around room, feet on ground
End with savasana (corpse pose) or seated stillness
Notice: what parts of body can you feel? Any numbness?
Practice cultivating sense of body as home, as safe

citations.author: Emerson, D. & Hopper, E.
citations.title: Overcoming Trauma through Yoga: Reclaiming Your Body

citations.author: Van der Kolk, B., et al.
citations.title: Yoga as an adjunctive treatment for posttraumatic stress disorder

strategies.contraindications: Not regular yoga - must be trauma-sensitive. Traditional yoga can be retraumatizing.

strategies.name: Bilateral Stimulation (Self-Applied)
strategies.description: Alternating left-right stimulation to activate both brain hemispheres, promoting processing and integration (EMDR-adjacent).
steps: Choose a method: butterfly hug, alternating knee taps, or eye movements
BUTTERFLY HUG: Cross arms over chest, hands on opposite shoulders
Alternately tap left shoulder, right shoulder, in a rhythm
Continue tapping while thinking about a mildly distressing memory
KNEE TAPS: Sit with hands on knees, alternately tap left knee, right knee
EYE MOVEMENTS: Hold up two fingers, move them left-right, follow with eyes
Do about 30-40 cycles of bilateral stimulation
Pause, take a breath, notice what you feel
If distress decreases, memory becomes less vivid, or insights arise - good
Repeat sets of bilateral stimulation until feeling settles
Not a replacement for EMDR therapy, but helpful for self-regulation
Best used with low-to-moderate intensity material, not severe trauma

citations.author: Shapiro, F.
citations.title: Efficacy of the eye movement desensitization procedure in the treatment of traumatic memories

strategies.contraindications: Do not use with severe trauma without professional support. May be activating.

strategies.name: Cold/Heat Therapy for Regulation
strategies.description: Use temperature to rapidly shift physiological state and regulate nervous system.
steps: COLD for high arousal/panic:
Splash cold water on face for 30 seconds
Or hold ice cube in hand/on forehead
Or take cold shower (start warm, end cold)
Cold activates 'dive reflex', rapidly calming nervous system
Breathe slowly while applying cold
HEAT for anxiety/disconnection:
Warm bath or shower
Heating pad on chest, abdomen, or back
Warm drink held in both hands
Heat promotes parasympathetic activation (rest & digest)
Notice body sensations with curiosity
Alternate cold and heat for resilience building
End with temperature that feels regulating for you

citations.author: Buijze et al.
citations.title: The Effect of Cold Showering on Health and Work

strategies.contraindications: Not for people with Raynaud's or cardiovascular issues without medical clearance

strategies.name: Somatic Tracking (Pain/Tension)
strategies.description: Bring mindful, safe attention to areas of physical pain or tension to support nervous system reprocessing.
steps: Find area of pain or tension in body
Instead of avoiding or bracing against it, turn toward it with curiosity
Rate intensity 0-10
Describe it without judgment: 'tight', 'hot', 'sharp', 'dull'
Remind yourself: 'This sensation is real, but I am safe'
Send breath to the area
Notice: does the sensation have edges? A center?
Notice: what happens if you imagine softening around it?
Stay with it for 1-2 minutes without trying to fix it
Notice any shifts: intensity changes, location moves, quality transforms
If intensity increases past 7/10, bring attention back to breath
Practice: sensation is information, not danger
Over time, nervous system learns it can be with sensation safely

citations.author: Schubiner, H. & Betzold, M.
citations.title: Unlearn Your Pain: A 28-Day Process to Reprogram Your Brain

citations.author: Gordon, A., et al.
citations.title: A psychological treatment for chronic back pain: a randomized trial

strategies.contraindications: Not for acute injury or medical pain requiring treatment. For chronic neuroplastic pain.

strategies.name: Grounding through Sensory Input
strategies.description: Use strong sensory experiences to bring awareness into body and present moment during dissociation or panic.
steps: Notice: Am I feeling spaced out, numb, or disconnected from my body?
Choose a STRONG sensory input:
TOUCH: Hold ice, snap rubber band on wrist, press feet firmly into floor
TASTE: Bite into lemon, eat strong mint, sour candy
SMELL: Strong scent - peppermint oil, coffee, vinegar
SOUND: Loud music, snap fingers near ears, clap hands
SIGHT: Look around room, name 5 objects and their colors
Engage the sense fully, with complete attention
Say out loud: 'I am here. I am in my body. This is now.'
Notice feet on ground, weight in chair, clothes on skin
Take 5 slow breaths, feeling breath move in and out
Check in: More present? Less spacey? If yes, you've grounded
If still dissociated, try a different sensory channel

citations.author: Najavits, L.
citations.title: Seeking Safety: A Treatment Manual for PTSD and Substance Abuse

meta.evidence_base: Somatic practices are increasingly supported by research in trauma treatment, chronic pain, and emotion regulation. Van der Kolk's work has demonstrated the necessity of body-based approaches for trauma recovery. Many somatic practices have clinical evidence; some have RCT support (trauma-sensitive yoga, cold therapy, somatic tracking).
meta.target_populations: Particularly valuable for trauma survivors, those with chronic pain, people who dissociate, and anyone who feels disconnected from their body. Also effective for stress, anxiety, and emotional dysregulation.
meta.core_principle: The body is not just where trauma is stored - it's also where healing happens. Developing a safe, friendly relationship with body sensations is foundational to emotional health.
meta.safety_note: Many somatic practices can be activating. Start slowly, have supports in place, and ideally work with a trauma-informed therapist when processing significant trauma. Always honor your body's signals to stop or slow down.
meta.training_note: While these can be practiced independently, training in Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or trauma-sensitive yoga provides deeper understanding. TRE certification exists for practitioners.
